conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess this, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have a child, Alex, who's 13 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To continue assessing your eligibility, I need to ask:

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't actually. I'm currently living abroad.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. To determine your eligibility while living abroad, I need to ask:

Are you working in the UK civil service, the armed forces, or for an organisation funded by the UK government?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not working for any of those organisations.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I need to ask one more important question:

Are you or your partner subject to immigration control, or do either of you have a residence condition that might affect your right to public funds in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of us are subject to immigration control or have any residence conditions affecting public funds.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for answering my questions. Based on the information you've provided, I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit** for Alex.

Here's why: While Alex meets the age requirement (being 13 years old and under 16), one of the key eligibility criteria for Child Benefit is that you must normally live in the UK. Since you're currently living abroad and are not working in the UK civil service, armed forces, or for a UK government-funded organisation (which are the exceptions that allow people living abroad to claim), you do not meet the residence requirement.

Unfortunately, this means you cannot claim Child Benefit while living outside the UK in your current circumstances.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 13 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

child_evaluations.reasoning: According to UK Child Benefit eligibility rules, to claim Child Benefit you must be responsible for a child under 16 (or under 20 if they stay in approved education or training) AND you must normally live in the UK. Alex is 13 years old, which meets the age requirement. However, the claimant is currently living abroad and is not working in the UK civil service, armed forces, or for a UK government-funded organisation - which are the exceptions that would allow someone living abroad to claim Child Benefit. Therefore, the claimant does not meet the residence requirement for Child Benefit eligibility for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has a 13-year-old child named Alex who meets the age criteria for Child Benefit. However, the claimant is living abroad and does not fall into any of the categories that would allow them to claim Child Benefit while living outside the UK (such as working in UK civil service, armed forces, or for a UK government-funded organisation). The fundamental residence requirement is not met, making the claimant ineligible for Child Benefit.
